name: Alviss
description: Alviss, whose name translates to 'All-Wise,' is a dwarf figure appearing in Norse mythology, primarily known from the poem *Alvíssmál* found within the *Poetic Edda*. He is portrayed as a suitor for Thor's daughter, Þrúðr. Alviss's claim to her hand stems from an agreement made by the gods, seemingly without Thor's consent. Thor, however, is unwilling to give his daughter to a dwarf. Instead of outright refusing, Thor challenges Alviss to a wisdom contest, demanding he answer every question posed to him. Alviss, confident in his extensive knowledge of the cosmos and various realms, readily accepts the challenge. Thor cleverly prolongs the questioning until dawn, at which point Alviss, being a dwarf, is petrified by the sunlight, thus preventing the marriage. The tale serves as an exploration of social hierarchies and the perceived differences between gods and dwarves. It also showcases Thor's cunning and his commitment to protecting his family from what he views as an unsuitable match.
subtitle: The All-Wise Dwarf

keyMyths.title: Alvíssmál: The Wisdom Contest
keyMyths.description: The primary myth surrounding Alviss is found within the poem *Alvíssmál*. Alviss arrives to claim Þrúðr, Thor's daughter, as his bride, based on a prior agreement. Thor, displeased, engages Alviss in a lengthy question-and-answer session, testing his knowledge of various realms and entities. Alviss provides detailed and comprehensive answers, demonstrating his vast wisdom. However, Thor intentionally draws out the contest until sunrise, knowing that dwarves are turned to stone by sunlight. Alviss is thus petrified before he can marry Þrúðr.
keyMyths.source: Larrington, Carolyne, translator. *The Poetic Edda*. Oxford University Press, 1996.

keyMyths.title: The Agreement for Þrúðr's Hand
keyMyths.description: Although the specifics are not elaborated upon, the *Alvíssmál* implies that an agreement was made between the gods (or at least some of them) and Alviss, promising him Þrúðr's hand in marriage. This agreement is what motivates Alviss to appear and claim his bride. The nature of this agreement, who made it, and why it was made are not fully explained, leaving room for interpretation and speculation about the political and social dynamics within the Norse pantheon and the relationships between gods and dwarves.

keyMyths.title: Alviss's Knowledge of Names
keyMyths.description: Throughout the *Alvíssmál*, Alviss demonstrates an exceptional understanding of the different names used by various beings (gods, men, dwarves, giants, elves) for the same things. This highlights the importance of language and perspective in Norse cosmology. His knowledge encompasses not just the names themselves but also the underlying cultural and societal contexts that shape them. This detailed awareness of linguistic variations is a key aspect of his 'all-wise' nature.
keyMyths.source: Byock, Jesse L., translator. *The Prose Edda*. Penguin Classics, 2005.
